name: Seaborgium
name_origin: Named in honor of American nuclear chemist Glenn T. Seaborg, who led the discovery of plutonium and many other transuranium elements.

discovery.discoverer: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory team (Albert Ghiorso et al.)

occurrence.abundance_summary: Seaborgium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators by fusing lighter and heavier nuclei (e.g., 18O on 249Cf).
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

compounds.name: Seaborgium dioxydichloride
compounds.summary: A volatile oxychloride identified in gas-phase chemistry experiments, analogous to MoO2Cl2 and WO2Cl2 and supporting the placement of Sg in group 6.

compounds.name: Seaborgium hexacarbonyl
compounds.summary: A metal hexacarbonyl synthesized in trace quantities, demonstrating that Sg can form an 18-electron carbonyl complex like its lighter homologs Mo and W.

reactivity_summary: Seaborgium is predicted and observed to behave as a group 6 transition metal, with chemistry comparable to molybdenum and tungsten, including a dominant +6 oxidation state and the ability to form volatile oxychlorides and carbonyl complexes.
isotopes_summary: All seaborgium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 269Sg with a half-life of about 14 minutes; shorter-lived isotopes such as 265Sg (half-life seconds to tens of seconds) are used in atom-at-a-time chemistry studies.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Seaborgium
Even, J. et al. 'Synthesis and detection of a seaborgium carbonyl complex', Science, 2014
